title: Kung Pao Chicken Sichuan
description: Experience the iconic 'ma la' (numbing and spicy) sensation with this authentic Sichuan Kung Pao Chicken. Tender, velveted chicken pieces are stir-fried with fiery dried chilies, fragrant Sichuan peppercorns, crunchy peanuts, and a balanced sweet-sour-savory sauce. It's a vibrant symphony of flavors and textures that truly represents the heart of Sichuan cuisine.

ingredients.notes: Snip into 1-inch pieces, remove most seeds
White parts sliced, green parts chopped for garnish
Such as peanut or grapeseed oil, divided

instructions.title: Prepare the Chicken Marinade
instructions.text: Cut the chicken thighs into 1-inch cubes. In a medium bowl, combine the chicken with 1 tablespoon light soy sauce, 1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine, 1.5 tablespoons cornstarch, 1 large egg white, and 1 tablespoon water. Mix well until the chicken is thoroughly coated and the marinade is absorbed. Set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes at room temperature, or up to 30 minutes in the refrigerator. This velveting technique ensures tender chicken.

instructions.title: Mix the Kung Pao Sauce
instructions.text: In a small bowl, whisk together the sauce ingredients: 1 tablespoon light soy sauce, 1 teaspoon dark soy sauce, 1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine, 2 tablespoons Chinkiang vinegar, 1.5 tablespoons granulated sugar, 1 tablespoon cornstarch, and 2 tablespoons chicken broth or water. Mix thoroughly until the cornstarch is dissolved and the sauce is uniform. Set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare Aromatics and Chilies
instructions.text: Snip the dried Sichuan chilies into 1-inch pieces, shaking out most of the seeds (keep a few for heat if desired). Mince the garlic and ginger. Slice the white parts of the green onions for stir-frying and chop the green parts for garnish. Ensure your roasted peanuts are ready. Have all ingredients prepped and within reach, as the stir-frying process is very quick.

instructions.title: Velvet and Sear the Chicken
instructions.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of neutral cooking oil in a wok or large skillet over medium-high heat. Once the oil is shimmering, add the marinated chicken in a single layer (you may need to do this in batches to avoid overcrowding). Sear for 2-3 minutes per side until lightly browned and mostly cooked through. Remove the chicken from the wok and set aside, leaving any residual oil in the wok.

instructions.title: Infuse the Oil with Spices and Aromatics
instructions.text: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the dried Sichuan chilies and whole Sichuan peppercorns to the wok. Stir-fry for about 30 seconds to 1 minute, until fragrant and the chilies turn a slightly darker shade. Be careful not to burn them. Immediately add the minced garlic, ginger, and sliced white parts of the green onions. Stir-fry for another 30 seconds until aromatic.

instructions.title: Combine and Finish the Dish
instructions.text: Return the seared chicken to the wok. Turn the heat back to high. Give the prepared sauce a quick whisk to re-combine any settled cornstarch, then pour it over the chicken and aromatics. Stir-fry vigorously for 1-2 minutes, allowing the sauce to thicken and coat all ingredients. Add the roasted peanuts and stir briefly to combine. Remove from heat immediately. Garnish with chopped green onion greens and serve hot with steamed white rice.

tips: **Mise en Place is Key:** This stir-fry cooks quickly, so have all your ingredients prepped and laid out before you start cooking.
**Wok Heat Management:** Use high heat for searing chicken and finishing, but lower it when infusing the oil with chilies and peppercorns to prevent burning.
**Don't Overcrowd the Wok:** Cook chicken in batches if necessary to ensure it sears properly and doesn't steam.

**Fresh Sichuan Peppercorns:** Use fresh, high-quality Sichuan peppercorns for the best numbing (ma) sensation. Toast them lightly in a dry pan before use for maximum fragrance, then crush them slightly.
**Adjust Spice Level:** For less heat, use fewer dried chilies or remove more seeds. For more heat, keep more seeds or add a touch of chili oil at the end.
**Authentic Chinkiang Vinegar:** Do not substitute Chinkiang black vinegar; its unique sweet and malty tang is crucial for the authentic flavor profile.
article:
## History of Kung Pao Chicken Sichuan

Kung Pao Chicken, known as Gong Bao Ji Ding (宫保鸡丁) in Mandarin, is arguably one of the most famous dishes to emerge from Sichuan cuisine. Its origins are often attributed to Ding Baozhen (1820–1886), a late Qing Dynasty official who served as the governor of Sichuan Province. The story goes that Ding, known for his culinary appreciation, either invented the dish himself or had his private chef create it based on a home-style recipe he enjoyed. The name "Gong Bao" (宫保) was his official title, meaning "Palace Guardian." The dish gained widespread popularity throughout China and eventually became a staple in Chinese restaurants worldwide, known for its distinctive "ma la" (numbing and spicy) flavor profile.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Kung Pao Chicken captures the true essence of Sichuan cooking through a careful balance of flavors and traditional techniques. The chicken is velveted with cornstarch and egg white, resulting in incredibly tender, succulent pieces that absorb the sauce beautifully. The signature "ma la" comes from the harmonious pairing of dried Sichuan chilies (for heat) and Sichuan peppercorns (for their unique citrusy, numbing sensation). The sauce, a blend of sweet, sour, salty, and savory notes from Shaoxing wine, Chinkiang vinegar, soy sauces, and sugar, perfectly complements the spicy elements. Finally, the crunch of roasted peanuts adds a delightful textural contrast, making each bite an exciting experience.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To achieve a truly restaurant-quality Kung Pao Chicken, precision in preparation and execution is paramount. Firstly, ensure your mise en place is flawless; all ingredients should be chopped, measured, and ready before you even turn on the stove, as stir-frying is a rapid process. Secondly, do not skip the velveting step for the chicken; it's the secret to its tender texture. When stir-frying the chilies and Sichuan peppercorns, keep the heat moderate to infuse the oil without burning the spices, which would result in a bitter taste. Finally, use a well-seasoned wok over high heat for the final stir-fry to achieve the coveted "wok hei" (breath of the wok), imparting a smoky, charred flavor that is characteristic of authentic Chinese stir-fries.

## Serving Suggestions

Kung Pao Chicken is a robust and flavorful dish that pairs wonderfully with simple accompaniments to let its complex taste shine. The most classic pairing is a bowl of perfectly steamed white rice, which helps to absorb the delicious sauce and balance the spiciness. For a more complete meal, consider serving it alongside a light, refreshing vegetable dish such as stir-fried bok choy with garlic or blanched gai lan (Chinese broccoli) with oyster sauce. A clear, light soup, like a simple egg drop soup, can also offer a soothing contrast to the bold flavors. For a truly authentic experience, enjoy it fresh off the wok, savouring the interplay of textures and the iconic "ma la" sensation.

Tender, velveted chicken pieces are stir-fried with fiery dried chilies, fragrant Sichuan peppercorns, crunchy peanuts, and a balanced sweet-sour-savory sauce. It's a vibrant symphony of flavors and textures that truly represents the heart of Sichuan cuisine.
jsonLd.keywords: Chinese, Chinese, authentic, traditional, chicken, Chinese, Sichuan, authentic, traditional, chicken, spicy, Kung Pao
recipeIngredient: 1.5 lbs (680g) Boneless, skinless chicken thighs (Cut into 1-inch cubes)
15-20 Dried Sichuan chilies (Snip into 1-inch pieces, remove most seeds)
3 stalks Green onions (White parts sliced, green parts chopped for garnish)
1/2 cup Roasted peanuts (Unsalted, skin on or off)
4 tbsp Neutral cooking oil (Such as peanut or grapeseed oil, divided)
1 tsp Dark soy sauce (For sauce (for color))
2 tbsp Chicken broth or water (For sauce)

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare the Chicken Marinade
recipeInstructions.text: Cut the chicken thighs into 1-inch cubes. Mix well until the chicken is thoroughly coated and the marinade is absorbed. Set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes at room temperature, or up to 30 minutes in the refrigerator. This velveting technique ensures tender chicken.

recipeInstructions.text: In a small bowl, whisk together the sauce ingredients: 1 tablespoon light soy sauce, 1 teaspoon dark soy sauce, 1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine, 2 tablespoons Chinkiang vinegar, 1.5 tablespoons granulated sugar, 1 tablespoon cornstarch, and 2 tablespoons chicken broth or water. Mix thoroughly until the cornstarch is dissolved and the sauce is uniform. Set aside.

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare Aromatics and Chilies
recipeInstructions.text: Snip the dried Sichuan chilies into 1-inch pieces, shaking out most of the seeds (keep a few for heat if desired). Mince the garlic and ginger. Slice the white parts of the green onions for stir-frying and chop the green parts for garnish. Ensure your roasted peanuts are ready. Have all ingredients prepped and within reach, as the stir-frying process is very quick.

recipeInstructions.text: Reduce the heat to medium. Add the dried Sichuan chilies and whole Sichuan peppercorns to the wok. Stir-fry for about 30 seconds to 1 minute, until fragrant and the chilies turn a slightly darker shade. Be careful not to burn them. Immediately add the minced garlic, ginger, and sliced white parts of the green onions. Stir-fry for another 30 seconds until aromatic.

recipeInstructions.text: Return the seared chicken to the wok. Turn the heat back to high. Give the prepared sauce a quick whisk to re-combine any settled cornstarch, then pour it over the chicken and aromatics. Garnish with chopped green onion greens and serve hot with steamed white rice.
